video_title: Volts ϟ 6.24.2026 – Ankit Kalanki (How To Keep People Cool Without Making The Planet Even Hotter)
gist: RMI's Ankit Kalanki argues cooling (AC + refrigeration) is a ~7% / soon-doubling share of global emissions and a faster-growing electricity load than data centers through 2030, but that ~97% of those emissions are addressable with existing tech. The bottleneck is commercialization plumbing — test standards that ignore humidity, lab readiness, supply chains and building/urban design — not invention, with desiccants and caloric solid-state cooling as the emerging frontier.

themes.theme: Cooling as an under-priced energy & emissions megatrend

insights.claim: Air conditioning and refrigeration produce roughly 7% of global greenhouse gas emissions today — comparable to global cement production — and that share could nearly double by 2050 if technology and growth trajectories don't change.
insights.speaker: David Roberts (host)
insights.so_what: Frames cooling as a cement-scale decarbonization vertical that is structurally under-covered versus data centers, sizing a long-duration capex and regulatory theme.
insights.quote: air conditioning and refrigeration — produces around 7% of global greenhouse gas emissions, as much as global cement production

insights.claim: The IEA now projects that AC electricity demand will grow faster than data center demand between now and 2030, with cooling already at 17% of global power consumption and projected to triple by 2050.
insights.speaker: David Roberts (host)
insights.so_what: If accurate, cooling — not AI — is the larger incremental power-demand driver this decade, a thesis for utilities, grid equipment, peaker capacity and cooling-efficiency plays.
insights.quote: the International Energy Agency (IEA) now projects AC electricity demand will grow faster than data center demand between now and 2030

insights.claim: Air conditioner ownership in emerging economies is climbing 10 to 15% per year, making the global south the dominant marginal market for cooling equipment and the place where efficiency standards lock in for decades.
insights.speaker: David Roberts (host)
insights.so_what: Points capacity, OEM share and policy leverage toward the global south; whatever standard wins there sets the installed-base efficiency for a generation.
insights.quote: where air conditioning ownership is climbing 10 to 15% a year, that share could nearly double by 2050

insights.claim: Cooling electricity consumption is projected to triple from ~5,000 TWh today (equal to total current US consumption) to ~18,000 TWh by 2050 — equivalent to the entire combined electricity use of the US, China, India, Japan and Germany.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the upper-bound demand shock for global generation, transmission and peak capacity if efficiency reforms fail — the bull case for cooling-efficiency and grid infrastructure.
insights.quote: tripling from 5,000 terawatt hours, which is the same as what the US consumes today, entirely, to 18,000 terawatt hours by 2050

insights.claim: Cooling drives a self-reinforcing 'doom loop' in which more cooling causes more warming, which raises cooling demand — making peak-day grid stress, not just annual energy, the binding constraint.
insights.speaker: David Roberts (host)
insights.so_what: Reinforcing feedback means cooling demand is convex to temperature, raising the value of peak-shaving efficiency, storage and demand-response in hot regions.
insights.quote: more cooling produces more warming, which raises demand for cooling, which produces more warming, and so on... it’s a bit of a doom loop

insights.claim: Roughly 97% of cooling emissions can be eliminated with existing technology — efficient ACs plus electricity decarbonization — meaning the gap is a deployment/policy problem rather than an R&D breakthrough problem.
insights.speaker: David Roberts (host)
insights.so_what: Investable edge sits in commercialization rails (standards, labs, supply chain, building retrofit) rather than moonshot cooling startups.
insights.quote: we already have the technology to cut cooling emissions by something like 97% — no tech breakthroughs required

themes.theme: Refrigerant transition: the perpetual round-trip back to natural gases

insights.claim: Today's dominant HFC refrigerants have on average ~2,000x the global warming potential of CO2, so even small leaks across hundreds of millions of units make refrigerant the second emissions channel of cooling alongside electricity.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Refrigerant GWP is the regulatory chokepoint driving forced product turnover; tracks demand for low-GWP refrigerants, leak-detection and recovery services.
insights.quote: these are now very high-potent greenhouse gases that have on average 2,000 times more global warming potential than carbon dioxide

insights.claim: The industry is reverting to natural refrigerants — propane (GWP ~3), ammonia, and CO2 (GWP 1) — completing a full circle back to where mechanical cooling started, but reintroducing flammability and toxicity trade-offs.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Natural-refrigerant transition reshuffles winners toward firms with safety engineering and handling expertise; favors hydrocarbon/CO2-compatible component makers.
insights.quote: In many parts of the world we are looking at hydrocarbons — one of the refrigerants is propane... which has a global warming potential of about 3

insights.claim: No ideal refrigerant exists that checks all boxes, so refrigerant choice is becoming application-specific rather than universal — outdoor/industrial setups can adopt natural refrigerants now with the right safety practices.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Fragmentation by application means no single refrigerant standard wins; bets should segment by use-case rather than chase one universal chemistry.
insights.quote: We have not found any ideal refrigerant to date that checks all our boxes

insights.claim: Despite refrigerant venting being legally banned in the US, very limited refrigerant recovery actually happens at end-of-life, so a large share of refrigerant still leaks during servicing and disposal.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Enforcement and incentive gap is a real addressable market for leak-detection, recovery, reclamation and technician-incentive schemes.
insights.quote: refrigerant venting is legally banned, there is very limited refrigerant recovery that happens at the end of life

insights.claim: Commercial facilities holding thousands of pounds of refrigerant are driving adoption of installed leak-detection systems that flag leaks in real time and trigger contractor repair.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Identifies a concrete commercial-sector product category (leak detection) with regulatory tailwinds in refrigerant-heavy facilities.
insights.quote: Installing leak detection systems is one intervention where a lot of work is happening

insights.claim: AC efficiency labels today rate only sensible (temperature) cooling and treat humidity removal as a byproduct, because air conditioners carry only a temperature sensor — meaning published ratings systematically misstate real-world efficiency in humid conditions.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: The single most actionable structural mispricing: when humidity rating enters standards, product rankings reshuffle and a re-tooling capex cycle begins.
insights.quote: It’s a purely sensible cooling-based approach to testing today. Humidity management is a byproduct

insights.claim: RMI field tests show that in humid conditions air conditioners use up to 30% more energy than their ratings imply, because users crank the thermostat down to chase dryness the rating never measures.
insights.speaker: David Roberts (host)
insights.so_what: Quantifies the rating-to-reality gap that justifies standard reform and exposes a hidden 30% efficiency upside an investor can underwrite.
insights.quote: air conditioners are using up to 30% more energy than you would think from their ratings

insights.claim: Better controls (smart management of temperature, humidity, compressor, refrigerant flow and fan speed) are the single biggest near-term lever to raise real-world AC efficiency and can be added using existing compressors and heat exchangers.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Controls/software is the lowest-capex efficiency lever — a software-margin play layered onto commodity hardware rather than a new supply chain.
insights.quote: That is, in my view, today’s biggest piece that needs to get solved for — that will significantly improve the efficiency of air conditioners

insights.claim: Upgrading only controls to respond to both temperature and humidity can deliver 15 to 20% energy savings 'right off the bat' using existing vendors, plausibly within a couple of years.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: A near-term, supply-chain-light 15-20% efficiency win is the most bankable first phase for OEMs and policymakers to capture quickly.
insights.quote: advancing controls by making your air conditioner better respond to both temperature and humidity can instantly deliver 15 to 20% savings right off the bat

insights.claim: RMI Global Cooling Prize prototypes have demonstrated 60 to 75% lower electricity use than typical ACs using only currently available technologies, yet none are buyable at retail because of how units are tested and rated.
insights.speaker: David Roberts (host)
insights.so_what: The 60-75% prototype-to-shelf gap is the core commercialization thesis — value accrues to whoever closes standards, labs and supply chain.
insights.quote: prototypes that have won those prizes have been 60 to 75% lower electricity use than typical ACs

insights.claim: Industry designs strictly to standards, so reform must start upstream at the rating method — once standards change, OEMs will produce efficient units, but not before, since they are cost-optimizing to today's compliance test.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Standards are the upstream control point; investors should track ISO/ASHRAE/BIS rulemaking as the trigger that unlocks the efficient-product cycle.
insights.quote: Industry designs products to standards and those standards get referenced everywhere

insights.claim: ISO is developing a new AC standard that shifts away from a capacity-based approach toward one rewarding better controls, expected to be fully published within a couple of years, after which national bodies (India's BIS, US DOE/AHRI) adapt it.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: A datable catalyst: ISO publication kicks off national adoption and the product re-tooling cycle — a watch item with a ~2-year horizon.
insights.quote: ISO is already working on a new standard... we are hopeful that in the next couple of years ISO will come out with a fully published standard

insights.claim: The incoming ASHRAE president has publicly elevated the overcooling/energy-waste issue in his presidential speeches, helping shift the engineering community toward humidity-aware standards.
insights.speaker: David Roberts (host)
insights.so_what: Institutional momentum signal — leadership endorsement de-risks the standards-reform thesis and shortens the path to adoption.
insights.quote: The ASHRAE president highlighted the overcooling issue and the energy waste associated with overcooling in his presidential speech

insights.claim: Adoption timing at the national level is gated by readiness of local labs and local manufacturers' testing capabilities, not by the standard itself — so RMI runs inter-laboratory testing to prove new methods are replicable across Japan, India and the US.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Lab/testing readiness is the hidden gating factor; testing-infrastructure and certification providers in emerging markets are an under-appreciated bottleneck play.
insights.quote: The readiness of the local labs and the local manufacturers' testing capabilities determines how soon and to what extent those standards will get adopted

insights.claim: The AC industry runs on very tight margins built on commodities — copper, aluminum and plastic — while being pushed to simultaneously raise efficiency, keep prices affordable, and keep supply chains domestic.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Thin-margin, commodity-exposed OEMs have little room to absorb efficiency mandates — implying pricing power shifts to controls/IP and component suppliers, and commodity sensitivity for the sector.
insights.quote: The air conditioning industry is not a very profit-making industry. The margins are very tight and they rely on commodities — copper, aluminum, plastic

insights.claim: Standards reform behaves like a coordination game where one OEM or buyer moving creates FOMO that pulls in others — so market-shaping intermediaries deliberately seed first-movers to build the policymaker confidence needed to expedite standards.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Adoption is non-linear and tipping-point driven; identifying the first-mover OEMs/buyers flags who captures early share when the standard flips.
insights.quote: when one industry player jumps in, it creates FOMO and other industry players also jump in

insights.claim: The compressor consumes the large majority of an AC's power and dictates peak demand — everything else is effectively plumbing — so compressor efficiency is the dominant determinant of grid impact.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Locates where the electricity (and therefore the cost and grid-stress) actually sits, focusing efficiency value on compressor/motor technology.
insights.quote: The compressor dictates how much power, and more particularly peak demand, air conditioning and heat pump contribute

insights.claim: India's residential AC market flipped from ~80% fixed-speed compressors in 2015 to ~80% inverter (variable-speed) technology by 2025, mirroring Japan, China (80-90%) — a completed efficiency transition in major markets.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: The inverter conversion is largely done in key Asian markets, so the next efficiency upside must come from controls and heat exchangers, not basic compressor type.
insights.quote: in India, the fixed speed market was almost 80% in 2015, and in 2025, it’s the opposite. 80% market is now inverter technology

insights.claim: Closing the path to super-efficient ACs is a phased journey — controls first (existing hardware), then compressor and heat-exchanger innovation plus regulatory updates — rather than a single leap to the 60-70% ceiling.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: A staged roadmap tells investors which lever monetizes first (controls) versus later (component R&D), sequencing capital deployment.
insights.quote: taking that first step of advancing controls... From there on, the journey goes on to advancing towards that 60 to 70% number

insights.claim: Building-side measures — shading, ventilation, insulation, better windows and reflective roofs — can cut the heat load enough to shrink the required AC, and in some climates eliminate the need for an air conditioner entirely.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Demand-avoidance via the building envelope is the cheapest 'cooling' — shifts value toward retrofit, insulation and code compliance markets.
insights.quote: you don't need air conditioning in the first place. Start with thinking about your building and how you can reduce the cooling need

insights.claim: Passive Daytime Radiative Cooling (PDRC) materials both reflect sunlight and radiate infrared heat through the atmosphere's narrow 8–15 (nm) window to outer space, letting a surface — and the building under it — drop below ambient temperature without electricity.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: A zero-electricity cooling mechanism; if durability is solved it competes with active cooling capex and is a distinct materials-startup category.
insights.quote: These PDRC materials have been tuned in a way... where they enable that heat to go into outer space through that narrow window

insights.claim: PDRC products remain pre-commercial — deployed as films on solar panels and paints in active testing by startups and RMI — with the gating need being durability evidence, not performance proof.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: PDRC is early-stage venture territory where durability data, not lab performance, is the binding milestone investors should diligence.
insights.quote: There is a lot of active testing happening of PDRC materials in different forms, as a film on solar panels, as a paint

insights.claim: Simple white-roof reflective cooling hasn't scaled despite low cost because of glare complaints, heat re-trapping between tall buildings in dense urban cores, and durability loss from dust, debris and rain — making it context-specific rather than universal.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Explains why an 'obvious' cheap solution stalls — durability and urban geometry, not price, are the real barriers shaping the addressable market.
insights.quote: the wear and tear — when it rains, if you live in an area where there is a lot of debris or dust, the effectiveness of that traditional white paint also gets impacted

insights.claim: Light-colored tiles and reflective paving blocks tested by RMI in Palawa City near Mumbai delivered 3 to 5 degrees Celsius of surface temperature reduction, demonstrating a deployable urban heat-island countermeasure.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: A field-proven 3-5C municipal intervention sized for procurement budgets — a concrete tender category for urban-resilience spending.
insights.quote: We have seen between 3 and 5 degrees Celsius of temperature change with light-colored tiles and reflective blocks

insights.claim: District cooling fits best in new, planned mixed-mode developments (residential/commercial/industrial) with consistent averaged cooling load, but is hard to retrofit into existing dense urban environments.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Defines the addressable niche for district cooling — greenfield planned developments in developing countries, not retrofits — narrowing where the model works.
insights.quote: in these new projects that are coming up, if you can plan them properly... district cooling can offer a good opportunity

insights.claim: District cooling realigns incentives via a cooling-as-a-service model: developers paid per unit of cooling delivered are motivated to invest in the most efficient systems, while consumers avoid upfront asset cost and pay only for cooling used.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: An efficiency-aligned business model (pay-per-cooling) that solves the upfront-cost barrier — a financing/operator opportunity distinct from equipment sales.
insights.quote: consumers don’t have to own the asset, they don’t have to worry about the upfront cost, they just have to pay for the cooling they use

insights.claim: Cooling centers, urban greening/tree planting, and technology-driven heat early-warning systems using weather data are becoming local-government priorities to protect outdoor workers from productivity loss and fatal heat exposure.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Municipal heat-resilience spending (warning systems, greening, cooling centers) is an emerging public-procurement theme tied to worker safety mandates.
insights.quote: Establishing cooling centers is another way where outdoor workers can go to these spaces that have some form of a cooling mechanism

insights.claim: Desiccant systems that bolt onto vapor-compression units to separately handle humidity let the AC stop overcooling air just to dehumidify it — directly attacking the latent-cooling energy waste and now backed by heavy startup activity.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Desiccants are the nearest-term frontier tech because they retrofit onto existing systems and monetize the documented humidity-waste gap.
insights.quote: the desiccant takes care of that humidity and the moisture removal... Your vapor compression system is only doing enough to reach the temperature

insights.claim: Amazon is commercially deploying desiccant-based cooling through a partnership with startup Transera, announced roughly two weeks before recording — signaling desiccants are crossing from lab to hyperscaler-scale deployment.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: A named hyperscaler commercial deployment is a hard de-risking datapoint for the desiccant category and Transera specifically.
insights.quote: Amazon partnering with a startup, Transera, to deploy their product was exciting to see

insights.claim: The make-or-break engineering challenge for desiccant cooling is regenerating the saturated desiccant efficiently — it requires a heat source, and doing it inefficiently just shifts the energy problem rather than solving it.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Regeneration efficiency is the key diligence question separating viable desiccant startups from those that merely relocate the energy cost.
insights.quote: How efficiently and innovatively you do that is what will determine the overall efficiency of the system when those desiccants are integrated

insights.claim: Solid-state caloric cooling — barocaloric, magnetocaloric and thermoelectric — uses pressure, magnetic or electric fields to drive a phase change in solid materials, eliminating both the refrigerant and the compressor that dominate AC electricity use.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Caloric cooling's elimination of refrigerant + compressor is the structural reason it could leapfrog vapor compression — the highest-upside, highest-risk venture frontier.
insights.quote: You don’t need a compressor, because you don’t have a refrigerant gas. That’s what makes it exciting and compelling in the context of the current refrigerant transition

insights.claim: Solid-state caloric cooling is still largely lab/prototype stage, with RMI-backed Magnotherm of Germany having built a magnetocaloric beverage/wine cooler — pointing to niche, low-load products as the first commercial entry point.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Names a specific early caloric player and signals the go-to-market beachhead (small appliances like wine/beverage coolers) before mainstream AC.
insights.quote: Magnotherm, from Germany, has developed a beverage cooler or a wine cooler using magnetocaloric technology

insights.claim: Caloric solid-state cooling's maintenance advantage over vapor compression is unproven — units still need pumps, fans, valves and heat exchangers to move the heat the solid material generates, so fewer-moving-parts claims are premature.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Tempers the bull narrative on caloric cooling — the reliability/maintenance edge is not yet demonstrated, a caution for early-stage valuations.
insights.quote: It's early to comment on that... because you still need pumps and mechanisms to move the fluid

themes.theme: Strategic outlook: incumbents, startups & the bull case

insights.claim: Kalanki argues short-to-medium-term effort should overwhelmingly target pushing vapor compression to its efficiency ceiling — not just raising the floor — because the entire industry, consumer, policy and standards ecosystem is built around it.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Signals where the near-term capital and returns concentrate — incremental vapor-compression efficiency — rather than speculative alternatives.
insights.quote: how do we get vapor compression technology to its ceiling and not just focus on raising the floor

insights.claim: Frontier cooling should target application-specific niches first — low-load products like wine coolers and vending machines are where novel tech can displace vapor compression before mainstream AC.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Tells investors to back novel cooling at the niche-appliance edge, not head-on against entrenched residential/commercial AC.
insights.quote: We do need a buckshot of solutions — not just one silver bullet of solutions

insights.claim: Rather than incumbents boxing out disruptors, the likely path is partnership — startups lack the scale, testing capability, certification network and voice that incumbent manufacturers possess, so the two must collaborate to scale new cooling tech.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Predicts a partnership/M&A dynamic over disruption — relevant for how cooling startups are valued and which exits are realistic.
insights.quote: Startups should think about how they can collaborate and partner with these manufacturers

insights.claim: The framing is 'not less cooling but better cooling' — cooling access is treated as a health-and-safety necessity, so policy will not suppress AC demand and the entire lever is efficiency and waste elimination, not demand destruction.
insights.speaker: Ankit Kalanki (guest, RMI)
insights.so_what: Confirms demand for cooling units stays structurally intact; the investable axis is efficiency/refrigerant/controls, not unit-volume contraction.
insights.quote: The goal is not about less cooling. We have to deliver better cooling
